document.critique: Rechecked capacities, all separation/same-machine rules, both exact-one conditions, and every implication. Candidate 1 is feasible and its costs sum to 166. Candidates 0 and 3 are feasible but cost 178 and 193; candidate 2 violates J3/J5 separation, and candidate 4 violates J2’s W/Z restriction. Retain the preceding proposal.

I tested all five candidates against capacities, restrictions, pairwise rules, conditional rules, and both exactly-one constraints. Candidate 0 fails the second exactly-one rule because J3 and J5 are both X; Candidates 1–3 are feasible but cost 188, 198, and 191. Candidate 4 is feasible and costs 176, not 157. Thus the preceding proposal should be retained with corrected exact cost 176.

Retested all machine counts, eligibility, pairwise constraints, implications, exact-one rules, and each cost. Candidate 0 survives every constraint with counts W/X/Y/Z = 3/3/3/3 and cost 155. Candidates 1–3 cost 164, 159, and 159, so the preceding proposal should be retained.

Tested all machine counts, costs, pairwise constraints, conditional rules, and exact-one conditions. Candidate 2 fails because J2 and J3 both use Z; candidates 0 and 1 survive but cost 209 and 214. Candidate 3 is feasible at 202, so the preceding proposal should be retained.

I re-evaluated all five candidates against the exact Boolean statements. None survives: the preceding proposal (Candidate 2) makes H’s statement true although H is labeled a liar, while every other candidate fails at least one statement (notably A or B). Therefore the supplied candidates and preceding proposal do not contain a consistent exact solution.

I recalculated the preceding proposal, including the disputed G and I statements. G’s outer XOR is false, matching G as a liar; I’s statement is true, matching I as truthful. I also checked J, which evaluates true, so J must remain truthful. Candidate 4 fails at E, while the preceding proposal survives all statement/type checks and should be retained.

I recomputed all 17 nested Boolean statements under the preceding proposal. Each statement’s value matches its speaker’s assigned type, including the less direct cases A, C, M, N, O, and P. I also tested the alternatives and found mismatches in each. Therefore Candidate 1 survives and the preceding proposal should be retained.

I independently evaluated all 17 statements under the preceding proposal. Every result matches its speaker’s type; in particular D’s XOR is true, so D must be truthful, while G’s full OR is false. The proposal survives unchanged and should be retained; Candidate 2 fails because it labels D a liar.

I independently rechecked the recurrence constraints across all 14 known equations for M ∈ {97,101,103} and all permitted coefficient ranges. The surviving system uses M=101 (including residue-0 values p[0]=−2, q[0]=−2, t=1, r=3), while the alternatives fail the modular equations. Forward evaluation yields 11, 34, 56, so the preceding proposal is retained.

Rechecking the phase-2 recurrence differences at n=10, 14, and 18, Candidate 0 survives: M=97, p2=1, q2=1, and t=-1 satisfy the resulting congruence. Candidate 2 does not satisfy this decisive constraint for any allowed modulus or coefficients. The preceding critique incorrectly rejected Candidate 0, so it should be retained.

I re-derived the residue-0 constraints using n=4,8,12,16. The correct congruences are 2+43p0−113q0−31t≡0 and −12−23p0+157q0+42t≡0. Exhaustive testing of allowed p0,q0,t and M leaves only (−1,−1,−1,103) from the first, which fails the second. Thus no valid system is verified, so the preceding [0,0,0] placeholder is retained.

I rechecked all recurrence equations for n=4 through 17 across M=97, 101, and 103, including the four residue classes, signed coefficient choices, r≠0, and normalized remainders. Candidates 0, 2, and 3 fail the constraints; candidate 1 alone admits a consistent unique system and continuation. I therefore retain [18,94,17].
